essence: Retail is a thin-margin, high-volume operating business: you make a few percent net, so scale, location, and inventory discipline are everything.
Like-for-like (same-store) growth + new-store expansion are the twin growth engines - and the first is the real health signal.
Inventory is the core risk and skill: buy right, turn fast; dead stock and markdowns kill profit.
Format and location economics decide success more than merchandise alone.
Omnichannel is now table-stakes; winners blend store + online + supply chain seamlessly.

definition.oneLiner: Businesses that sell goods directly to consumers through stores and/or online, aggregating assortment, convenience and price.
definition.jobToBeDone: Get the right products to consumers conveniently and affordably - selection, availability, and a good buying experience.

valueChain.profitPools: Profit pools sit with scaled, well-located formats and efficient supply chains; private label and high-margin categories lift the mix, while discount-heavy categories run razor-thin; loyalty data and retail-media increasingly add margin.
valueChain.powerShift: Value is shifting from pure brick-and-mortar -> omnichannel + supply-chain excellence -> data, private label and retail-media, as e-commerce and quick-commerce compress traditional store economics.

unitEconomics.unitOfRevenue: Revenue per store (and per sq ft), plus online revenue per order
unitEconomics.revenueDrivers: Stores x Sales-per-store. Growth = like-for-like (same-store) growth + new-store additions + online; basket size and footfall drive same-store.

costStructure.shape: Cost of goods dominates (a buy-sell business), then rent/store opex and staff; net margins are thin (low single digits), so inventory turns and shrinkage/markdown control decide profitability.
costStructure.leverage: Operating leverage from scale (buying power, supply-chain fixed costs) and same-store growth over a fixed base; but high fixed rent/staff means sales declines hurt fast.

competitionAndMoats.concentration: Varies by category and geography - some consolidated (grocery), many fragmented (India still has huge unorganised retail); e-commerce intensifies competition everywhere.

structuralForces.regulation: Moderate-to-heavy - FDI rules (esp. multi-brand retail in India), zoning/real-estate, labour laws, consumer-protection and (online) e-commerce rules.
structuralForces.cyclicality: Discretionary categories swing with consumer confidence; grocery is more defensive; seasonal peaks matter hugely.
structuralForces.substitution: E-commerce, quick-commerce, D2C brands, and unorganised/local retail all substitute; channels blur.
structuralForces.supplierBuyerPower: Large retailers wield strong power over suppliers (and push private label); consumers have near-infinite choice and low switching costs - power sits with the consumer.

aiPenetration.maturity: Medium-to-high - demand forecasting, pricing, and personalisation are widely used; in-store AI and agentic shopping emerging.
aiPenetration.valueChainImpact: AI's biggest wins are in inventory (the core risk) and pricing/markdowns, plus personalisation that lifts basket and loyalty; retail-media (AI-targeted ads) is a high-margin new revenue line.
opportunities: Lower markdowns & stockouts via better forecasting

ranges: Inventory turns: higher is better (grocery high, fashion lower)
Like-for-like growth: low-to-mid single digits is healthy
benchmarks.note: DIRECTIONAL - rules of thumb, not audited figures. Verify before quoting.

keyTerms.Like-for-like (LFL) / same-store sales: Sales growth from stores open >1 year - the true organic health metric.
keyTerms.Inventory turnover: How many times stock is sold and replaced per year - higher = more efficient.
keyTerms.Markdown: Price cut to clear unsold stock - a direct margin hit.
keyTerms.Shrinkage: Inventory lost to theft, damage, or error.
keyTerms.Private label: Retailer's own-brand products - higher margin than national brands.
keyTerms.Footfall / conversion: Store visitors, and the % who buy.
keyTerms.Omnichannel: A seamless blend of store, online, and fulfilment.

questionsToAskFirst: What's like-for-like sales growth (vs new-store growth)?
How healthy are inventory turns and markdowns?
What's the format/location economics and new-store ROI?
rookieTraps: Masking weak same-store performance with new-store openings
Chasing online growth that loses money on delivery
Underestimating rent/labour operating leverage in a downturn

whatsUniqueHere: Retail is an unforgiving operating business: net margins are so thin that the winners aren't those with the best products but those who run the tightest machine - buying right, turning inventory fast, and squeezing cost out of a sprawling store-and-supply-chain network. Growth quality matters more than growth: same-store growth is real health; opening stores to mask flat productivity is the classic trap.

indiaLandscape.marketStructure: One of the world's largest retail markets, still ~85%+ unorganised (kirana), with organised retail growing fast: Reliance Retail (the giant), Avenue Supermarts (DMart), Tata (Trent / Croma / BigBasket), Aditya Birla Fashion; e-commerce and quick-commerce reshaping the landscape.
indiaLandscape.note: Semi-perishable - players & policy specifics refresh periodically.

similarTo: Internet & E-commerce - selection, supply chain, thin margins

currentState.note: PERISHABLE - refresh via research before quoting. Figures approximate.
currentState.marketStructure: India retail: Reliance Retail the scale leader; DMart the value/efficiency benchmark; Tata building an omnichannel ecosystem; quick-commerce eating into grocery trips; organised retail steadily taking share from kirana while ONDC tries to level the field.
